ACTION:
<answer>
<procedure>MAKESOLUTION (Solution A) with [MOL] ```CCCOc1c(CNC)cccc1OC``` [/MOL] (115 mg, 0.55 mmol) and [MOL] ```CN(C)C=O``` [/MOL] (5 mL)</procedure>

<procedure>ADD [MOL] ```CCN(C(C)C)C(C)C``` [/MOL] (261 uL, 1.5 mmol)</procedure>
<procedure>ADD [MOL] ```CCN=C=NCCCN(C)C``` [/MOL] (110 mg, 0.55 mmol)</procedure>
<procedure>MICROWAVE for <time>5 min</time> at <temp>130 C</temp></procedure>
<procedure>SETTEMP to <temp>0 C</temp></procedure>
<procedure>ADD [MOL] ```O``` [/MOL]</procedure>

<procedure>TRITURATE with diethyl ether</procedure>

</answer>
SOURCE: To a solution of (3-methoxy-2-propoxy-benzyl)-methyl-amine (115 mg, 0.55 mmol) in DMF (5 mL) were added in sequential order 3-(2,2-dimethyl-3-oxo-3,4-dihydro-2H-pyrido[3,2-b][1,4]oxazin-7-yl)-acrylic acid (181 mg, 0.5 mmol), 1-hydroxybenzotriazole (74 mg, 0.55 mmol), diisopropylethylamine (261 uL, 1.5 mmol), and N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide (110 mg, 0.55 mmol). The reaction was placed in a microwave at 130° C. for 5 min. The solution was cooled in an ice bath and water was added with rapid stirring. The precipitate was filtered, dried and triturated with diethyl ether to yield the title compound (164 mg, 75%) as a white solid and as a mixture of amide rotomers: 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d6) δ 11.37-11.39 (m, 1H), 8.14-8.18 (m, 1H), 7.87-7.93 (m, 1H), 7.47-7.53 (m, 1H), 7.27-7.34 (m, 1H), 6.92-7.09 (m, 3H), 6.60-6.66 (m, 1H), 4.62-4.79 (m, 2H), 3.84-3.90 (m, 2H), 3.78 (s, 3H), 2.88-3.09 (m, 3H), 1.70-1.74 (m, 2H), 1.40-1.49 (m 6H), 0.93-0.99 (m, 3H); MS (ESI) m/e 440 (C24H29N3O5+H)+.
Thinking_text: This transformation is likely an amide coupling reaction between a secondary amine, (3-methoxy-2-propoxy-benzyl)-methyl-amine, and an acrylic acid derivative, 3-(2,2-dimethyl-3-oxo-3,4-dihydro-2H-pyrido[3,2-b][1,4]oxazin-7-yl)-acrylic acid, to form an enamide product. The reaction probably employs a carbodiimide-based coupling strategy, utilizing N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) as the coupling agent and 1-hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBt) as an additive to suppress racemization and enhance efficiency. Diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) serves as a base to deprotonate the acid and maintain a suitable reaction pH. DMF is chosen as the polar aprotic solvent to solubilize all components. Microwave irradiation at 130°C likely accelerates the activation of the carboxylic acid and subsequent nucleophilic attack by the amine, with the conjugated acrylate system potentially influencing reactivity through electronic effects. Workup involves quenching with water to precipitate the product, followed by filtration, drying, and trituration with diethyl ether to remove impurities and yield the solid enamide. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: prepare a solution of the amine in DMF; sequentially add the acid, HOBt, DIPEA, and EDC; heat under microwave irradiation; cool; add water to precipitate; filter; dry; triturate with ether; and isolate the product.

<procedure>MAKESOLUTION (Solution A) with [MOL] ```N#Cc1cn(CCl)c2ccccc12``` [/MOL] (1.70 g, 8.98 mmol) and [MOL] ```N#CC(C#N)CCC(F)(F)F``` [/MOL] (1.45 g, 7.48 mmol) in [MOL] ```CN(C)C=O``` [/MOL] (27 ml)</procedure>
<procedure>ADD [MOL] ```O=C([O-])[O-].[K+].[K+]``` [/MOL] (2.49 g, 18.0 mmol) to Solution A</procedure>
<procedure>STIR for <time>7 hours</time> at <temp>room temperature</temp></procedure>
<procedure>ADD [MOL] ```O``` [/MOL] (excess)</procedure>
<procedure>EXTRACT with MTBE</procedure>
<procedure>COLLECTLAYER organic</procedure>
<procedure>WASH with [MOL] ```O``` [/MOL]</procedure>
<procedure>DRYSOLUTION over magnesium sulfate</procedure>
<procedure>FILTER keep filtrate</procedure>
<procedure>CONCENTRATE</procedure>
<procedure>YIELD [MOL] ```N#Cc1cn(CC(C#N)(C#N)CCC(F)(F)F)c2ccccc12``` [/MOL] (1.48 g, 3.36 mmol, 45% yield)</procedure>
</answer>
SOURCE: 1.70 g of 1-(chloromethyl)-3-cyano-1H-indole and 1.45 g of (3,3,3-trifluoropropyl) malononitrile were dissolved in 27 ml of N,N-dimethylformamide. 2.49 g of potassium carbonate was added to the solution under ice cooling with stirring, followed by stirring at room temperature for 7 hours. Water was added to the reaction mixture, and then extracted with MTBE. The organic layer was washed with water, dried over anhydrous magnesium sulfate, filtered, and concentrated under reduced pressure. The residue was subjected to silica gel column chromatography to obtain 1.48 g of [{3-cyano-1H-indole-1-yl}methyl] (3,3,3-trifluoropropyl) malononitrile (referred to as the compound of the present invention (39), hereinafter) shown by below formula.
Thinking_text: This transformation is a nucleophilic substitution (likely SN2) where the chloromethyl group on the indole nitrogen is displaced by the carbanion generated from (3,3,3-trifluoropropyl)malononitrile. The malononitrile derivative, with two strongly electron-withdrawing cyano groups, forms a stabilized carbanion upon deprotonation by potassium carbonate, a mild base. The reaction proceeds via attack of this nucleophile on the electrophilic carbon of the chloromethyl moiety. The choice of DMF as solvent is appropriate due to its polar aprotic nature, which enhances nucleophilicity without interfering with the base. The room temperature and extended stirring time (7 hours) suggest moderate reactivity, possibly due to steric hindrance around the chloromethyl site or the stability of the carbanion. Workup involves quenching with water, extraction with MTBE (a good solvent for organic products), washing to remove polar impurities, drying, and concentration. The moderate yield (45%) may result from competing side reactions or incomplete conversion. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: dissolve reactants in DMF; add potassium carbonate; stir at room temperature; quench with water; extract with MTBE; wash organic layer; dry; concentrate; and isolate product.

</answer>
SOURCE: A mixture of 4,6-dichloro-5-methyl-thieno[2,3-d]pyrimidine (150 mg), [4-(3-amino-pyridin-2-yloxy)-cyclohexyl]-methyl-carbamic acid tert-butyl ester (265 mg) and p-toluenesulfonic acid (20 mg) in dioxane (5 ml) was stirred at 100° C. for 2 days. The reaction mixture was concentrated in vacuo and stirred with 25% TFA in DCM for 2 hours at ambient temperature. Then the reaction mixture was concentrated in vacuo again and purified by RP-chromatography ((H2O+2% TFA)/MeOH: gradient 85/15 to 0/100). The fractions were collected, concentrated in vacuo and the residue was dissolved in DCM/MeOH and 1 M sodium hydroxide solution. The organic layer was dried and concentrated in vacuo to yield the title compound.
Thinking_text: This transformation likely involves a nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) followed by a deprotection. The reactant 4,6-dichloro-5-methyl-thieno[2,3-d]pyrimidine contains two chlorine atoms on the pyrimidine ring, which are susceptible to nucleophilic attack by the primary amine group of [4-(3-amino-pyridin-2-yloxy)-cyclohexyl]-methyl-carbamic acid tert-butyl ester. The electron-withdrawing nature of the pyrimidine ring activates the chlorides for substitution, with the 4-position likely being more reactive due to less steric hindrance compared to the 6-position adjacent to the thiophene ring. p-Toluenesulfonic acid probably catalyzes the reaction by protonating the pyrimidine nitrogen, enhancing its electrophilicity. Dioxane is a suitable solvent due to its high boiling point and ability to dissolve both reactants. The prolonged heating at 100°C is necessary for the SNAr, which can be slow. The subsequent treatment with TFA in DCM removes the tert-butyloxycarbonyl (Boc) protecting group from the carbamate, yielding the free secondary amine. The use of 25% TFA in DCM is standard for Boc deprotection under mild conditions. Basic workup with NaOH neutralizes excess acid and facilitates extraction of the product into the organic layer. Reverse-phase chromatography with a TFA-modified mobile phase suggests the product may be polar and basic, requiring ion-pairing for purification. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: SNAr coupling in dioxane with acid catalysis, Boc deprotection with TFA, basic aqueous workup, and purification via reverse-phase chromatography.

</answer>
SOURCE: To (S)-4-(4-fluorophenyl)-3-(3-(4-(1-((2-(trimethylsilyl)ethoxy)methyl)-1H-pyrazol-3-yl)phenyl)pyrazolo[1,5-a]pyrimidin-5-yl)oxazolidin-2-one (80 mg, 0.14 mmol) in DCM (4 mL) was added TFA (1 mL) and the reaction was stirred for 4 hours at ambient temperature. The reaction was concentrated to dryness, and 7N ammonia in methanol was added to the residue. After concentration to dryness, the residue was purified by reverse phase chromatography (SP4, 12M, water/CH3CN 100:0 to 0:100, 20 column volumes) to yield (S)-3-(3-(4-(1H-pyrazol-3-yl)phenyl)pyrazolo[1,5-a]pyrimidin-5-yl)-4-(4-fluorophenyl)oxazolidin-2-one (35 mg, 57% yield) as a white solid. LCMS (APCI+) m/z 441.2 [M+H]+.
Thinking_text: This transformation involves the deprotection of a SEM (2-(trimethylsilyl)ethoxymethyl) group from a pyrazole nitrogen, converting the reactant to the product. The reaction is an acid-catalyzed deprotection, where trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) likely protonates the SEM ether oxygen, facilitating cleavage of the N-O bond to release the free pyrazole (1H-pyrazole). The solvent dichloromethane (DCM) is suitable due to its ability to dissolve both the organic substrate and TFA while being inert. After the deprotection, the reaction is concentrated to remove volatile components, and ammonia in methanol is added to quench excess acid and neutralize the medium, preventing potential side reactions. The final concentration yields the crude product, which requires purification (as indicated in the source, though not in the atomic action sequence) via reverse-phase chromatography to isolate the target compound. The stereochemistry at the oxazolidinone is retained as it is not involved in the transformation. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: dissolve the SEM-protected compound in DCM; add TFA; stir at room temperature; concentrate; treat with ammonia in methanol; concentrate; and purify to obtain the product.

SOURCE: To a stirred warm (60° C.) solution of ethyl 4-amino-1-piperidinecarboxylate (86.1 mg, 0.5 mmol) and paraformaldehyde (16 mg, 0.5 mmol) in methanol (2 mL) was added N-(3,4-dichloro-benzyl)-2-(2,2-dimethyl-5-oxo-[1,3]dioxolan-4-ylidene)-N-methyl-acetamide (175 mg, 0.5 mmol). After 2 h, the reaction mixture was cooled and purified using preparative HPLC on a C18 column using water/methanol-(0.1% TFA) as the eluent. The fractions containing the desired product were combined, concentrated and lyophilized to give the title compound as a white powder (64 mg, 27% yield). 1HNMR (500 MHz, CDCl3) δ: 10.16 (2H, br s), 7.40 (1H, d, J=8.2 Hz), 7.35 (1H, s), 7.10 (1H, d, J=8.0 Hz), 4.59 (2H, s), 4.34–4.18 (3H, m), 4.13 (2H, q, J=7.0 Hz), 4.09 (2H, s), 2.06 (2H, s), 2.87–2.80 (2H, br m), 1.82–1.76 (2H, br m), 1.63–1.55 (2H, m), 1.25 (3H, t, J=7.0 Hz). MS calcd for C21H26Cl2N3O5 (M+H): 470.36. found: 470.02.
Thinking_text: This transformation likely involves a multicomponent Mannich-type reaction or aza-Michael addition, followed by intramolecular cyclization. The reactant ethyl 4-amino-1-piperidinecarboxylate provides a primary amine nucleophile, paraformaldehyde serves as a formaldehyde source for imine formation, and N-(3,4-dichlorobenzyl)-2-(2,2-dimethyl-5-oxo-[1,3]dioxolan-4-ylidene)-N-methyl-acetamide contains an activated α,β-unsaturated carbonyl (likely as a Meldrum's acid derivative) acting as a Michael acceptor. The reaction probably proceeds via initial imine formation between the amine and formaldehyde, followed by nucleophilic attack of the imine on the electron-deficient alkene of the Meldrum's acid derivative, leading to C–C bond formation. The Meldrum's acid moiety then undergoes ring-opening and decarboxylation under the reaction conditions, facilitating cyclization to form the observed bicyclic lactam product. Methanol is chosen as a polar protic solvent to solubilize all components and potentially stabilize intermediates through hydrogen bonding. The moderate temperature of 60°C is optimal to drive the multistep sequence without causing excessive decomposition. Workup involves simple concentration, likely due to the product's low solubility, with purification implied via methods like HPLC (as per source) to remove polar impurities. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: prepare a warm solution of the amine and formaldehyde in methanol; add the Michael acceptor; stir at 60°C for 2 hours; concentrate; and purify to isolate the product.

</answer>
SOURCE: A mixture of 5.00 g (28.2 mmol) of 2-dimethylaminobenzo[b]thiophene (Vesterager et al., Tetrahedron, 1973, 29, 321-329) and 6.3 g (33.9 mmol) of 4-nitrobenzoyl chloride in 100 mL of chlorobenzene was heated at 105° C. for 6 h. The reaction was cooled and concentrated in vacuo. Purification of the residue by flash chromatography (SiO2; 5% then 10% then 25% EtOAc in hexanes) afforded 7.51 g (23.0 mmol; 82%) of the title compound as burgundy flakes.
Thinking_text: This transformation is likely an acylation reaction, specifically a nucleophilic acyl substitution, where the aromatic amine group of 2-dimethylaminobenzo[b]thiophene attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of 4-nitrobenzoyl chloride, displacing chloride to form an amide bond. The dimethylamino group is electron-donating, enhancing the nucleophilicity of the adjacent aromatic ring, while the nitro group on the benzoyl chloride is electron-withdrawing, increasing the electrophilicity of the carbonyl. Chlorobenzene is chosen as the solvent due to its high boiling point (≈130°C), which accommodates the elevated reaction temperature (105°C) needed to drive the substitution, and its inertness under these conditions. No catalyst is required as the amine is sufficiently nucleophilic. After heating, the mixture is concentrated to remove the solvent, and the crude product likely requires purification via chromatography to separate the amide from any unreacted starting materials or byproducts, given the presence of polar nitro and amide groups. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: dissolve the amine in chlorobenzene, add the acid chloride, heat with stirring, concentrate, and purify by chromatography.

SOURCE: 7-chloro-2-(4-methoxybenzyl)-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-2H-pyrazolo[4,3-d]pyrimidine (0.16 mmol) and 4-(4-cyclopropylpiperazin-1-yl)aniline (0.3 mmol 2 eq.) were suspended in MeOH (dry, 3 mL) in a microwave vial (2-5 mL), HCl in dioxane (4M, 3 drops) was added. The reaction mixture was irradiated in a microwave reactor for 5 min at 140° C. The reaction mixture was evaporated and used without further purification. The residue was dissolved in TFA (3 mL). The reaction mixture was irradiated in a microwave reactor for 5 min at 140° C. The reaction mixture was concentrated and purified by semi-preparative HPLC-MS and freeze dried from water/t-BuOH 4/1.
Thinking_text: This transformation likely involves a nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) followed by an acid-mediated deprotection or cyclization. The reactant is a 7-chloro-pyrazolopyrimidine derivative, where the chlorine at the 7-position is activated by the electron-deficient pyrimidine ring and the adjacent nitrogen atoms, making it susceptible to nucleophilic attack. The nucleophile is a 4-(4-cyclopropylpiperazin-1-yl)aniline. The aniline nitrogen, being a good nucleophile, can displace the chlorine via an addition-elimination mechanism, facilitated by the electron-withdrawing nature of the heterocycle. The initial step likely uses methanol as a polar protic solvent to dissolve both reactants, with catalytic HCl in dioxane to protonate the pyrimidine nitrogen, further enhancing its electrophilicity. Microwave irradiation at 140°C accelerates the SNAr by providing efficient heating. The subsequent treatment with trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) is intriguing. The product structure shows loss of the 4-methoxybenzyl (PMB) group from the reactant's N2 position and formation of an NH group in the final pyrazole ring. Therefore, the TFA step likely serves a dual purpose: it cleaves the PMB protecting group (an acid-labile benzyl ether) and may also facilitate a final cyclization or tautomerization to yield the observed [1,2,4]triazolo[4,3-a]pyrazine core. The use of strong acid like TFA under microwave conditions ensures complete deprotection. Concentration and drying remove volatile acids and solvents, while filtration likely removes any insoluble salts or byproducts before the final product is isolated, presumably via the mentioned HPLC and lyophilization. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: prepare a mixture of the chloro-heterocycle and aniline in methanol; add catalytic HCl/dioxane; heat under microwave; concentrate; treat with TFA under microwave; concentrate; dry; filter; and purify.

SOURCE: To a solution of a mixture of 1-(benzyloxy)-2,3-difluoro-4-nitrobenzene and 2-(benzyloxy)-3,4-difluoro-1-nitrobenzene (6.54 g) in methanol (200 ml) was added 10% palladium on carbon (654 mg), followed by stirring under a hydrogen atmosphere at room temperature for 26 hr and 50 min. The atmosphere in the reaction vessel was replaced with nitrogen to stop the reaction, and the catalyst was filtered off through Celite. The filtrate was concentrated under reduced pressure to provide the titled compound as a black solid (3.52 g) which was a mixture of 6-amino-2,3-difluorophenol.
Thinking_text: This transformation involves the reduction of nitro groups to amines, likely via catalytic hydrogenation using palladium on carbon (Pd/C) as a heterogeneous catalyst. The reactants are two regioisomeric benzyloxy-difluoro-nitrobenzenes, each containing a nitro group (-NO2) attached to an aromatic ring with fluorine and benzyloxy substituents. The reaction proceeds through adsorption of the nitro compound onto the Pd surface, followed by hydrogenation to form the corresponding amine (-NH2), with concurrent cleavage of the benzyl ether protecting group to yield a phenol. Methanol serves as a polar protic solvent, facilitating hydrogen solubility and substrate dissolution. The presence of electron-withdrawing fluorine atoms likely enhances the electrophilicity of the nitro group, promoting reduction. After reaction completion under a hydrogen atmosphere at room temperature, quenching with nitrogen halts hydrogenation. Filtration removes the solid catalyst, and concentration under reduced pressure isolates the crude product, a mixture of 6-amino-2,3-difluorophenol isomers, due to the regioisomeric starting materials. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: prepare a solution of the nitro compounds in methanol; add Pd/C catalyst; stir under hydrogen at room temperature; quench with nitrogen; filter; concentrate; and yield the amino-phenol product.

<procedure>YIELD [MOL] ```CC(C)(C)OC(=O)N1CCN(C(=O)c2ncn(CC3(O)CCN(C(=O)c4ccccc4)CC3)c2-c2ccccc2)[C@H](Cc2ccccc2)C1``` [/MOL] (110 mg).</procedure>
</answer>
SOURCE: tert-Butyl (3R)-3-benzyl-4-({1-[(1-benzyl-4-hydroxypiperidin-4-yl)methyl]-5-phenyl-1H-imidazol-4-yl}carbonyl)piperazine-1-carboxylate (125 mg) and triethylamine (2 ml) were dissolved in THF (3.5 ml), benzoyl chloride (38 mg) was added and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2 hr. The reaction mixture was poured into aqueous sodium bicarbonate solution and extracted with ethyl acetate. The extract was washed with brine, and dried over anhydrous sodium sulfate, and the solvent was evaporated in vacuo. The residue was subjected to silica gel column chromatography, and the fraction eluted with ethyl acetate-hexane (1:1) was concentrated in vacuo to give the desired product (110 mg) as an amorphous solid.
Thinking_text: This transformation involves the acylation of a secondary amine, likely the piperazine nitrogen, with benzoyl chloride. The reactant contains a complex scaffold with a piperazine ring bearing a Boc-protected amine and a secondary amine adjacent to a hydroxypiperidine moiety. The nucleophilic secondary amine attacks the electrophilic carbonyl of benzoyl chloride, facilitated by the base triethylamine which scavenges the generated HCl. The reaction likely proceeds via a nucleophilic acyl substitution mechanism. THF is a suitable aprotic polar solvent that dissolves both organic reactants and facilitates the reaction without interfering. The presence of the base is crucial to deprotonate the amine and drive the equilibrium toward product formation. After the reaction, quenching with aqueous sodium bicarbonate neutralizes any excess acid, and extraction with ethyl acetate isolates the organic product. Washing with brine removes residual water-soluble impurities, and drying over sodium sulfate ensures an anhydrous organic solution prior to concentration. The product is a benzamide derivative, and the yield suggests moderate efficiency, possibly due to steric hindrance from the bulky substituents. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: dissolve the amine substrate and base in THF, add benzoyl chloride, stir at room temperature, quench with aqueous sodium bicarbonate, extract with ethyl acetate, wash with brine, dry over sodium sulfate, concentrate, and isolate the product.

ACTION:
<answer>
<procedure>MAKESOLUTION (Solution A) with [MOL] ```CC(C)NC(C)C``` [/MOL] (1.04 mL, 7.38 mmol) and [MOL] ```C1CCOC1``` [/MOL] (13.4 mL)</procedure>
<procedure>SETTEMP <temp>-78 C</temp></procedure>
<procedure>ADD [MOL] ```[Li]CCCC``` [/MOL] (3.4 mL, 8.39 mmol) dropwise</procedure>
<procedure>STIR for <time>5 min</time></procedure>
<procedure>ADD [MOL] ```CC#N``` [/MOL] (0.386 mL, 7.38 mmol)</procedure>
<procedure>STIR for <time>10 min</time></procedure>
<procedure>MAKESOLUTION (Solution B) with [MOL] ```CC1(C)CCCOC1=O``` [/MOL] (0.860 g, 6.71 mmol) and [MOL] ```C1CCOC1``` [/MOL] (6.71 mL)</procedure>
<procedure>ADD Solution B to Solution A</procedure>
<procedure>STIR for <time>6 h</time> while warming to <temp>5 C</temp></procedure>
<procedure>QUENCH with [MOL] ```[Cl-].[NH4+]``` [/MOL] (aq)</procedure>
<procedure>PARTITION with ethyl acetate</procedure>
<procedure>COLLECTLAYER organic</procedure>
<procedure>WASH with brine</procedure>
<procedure>DRYSOLUTION over MgSO4</procedure>
<procedure>FILTER keep filtrate</procedure>
<procedure>CONCENTRATE</procedure>
<procedure>YIELD [MOL] ```CC(C)(CCCO)C(=O)CC#N``` [/MOL] (578 mg)</procedure>
</answer>
SOURCE: To a solution of N-isopropylpropan-2-amine (1.04 mL, 7.38 mmol) in tetrahydrofuran (13.4 mL) at −78° C., was slowly added a solution of n-butyllithium (3.4 mL, 8.39 mmol). This mixture was stirred for 5 min before adding acetonitrile (0.386 mL, 7.38 mmol) and 10 min later a solution 3,3-dimethyltetrahydropyran-2-one (0.860 g, 6.71 mmol) in THF (6.71 mL). The reaction was then left to slowly warm up and was quenched with NH4Cl after 6 h (˜5° C.). The reaction was left sitting at rt overnight. The aqueous layer was then extracted with EtOAc. The combined organic layers were washed with brine, dried over MgSO4 and concentrated. (578 mg) 1H NMR (CDCl3) 3.92 (dt, 1H); 3.70 (dd, 1H); 2.73 (d, 1H); 2.67 (d, 1H); 2.42 (br s, 1H); 1.92 (dt, 1H); 1.88-1.77 (m, 1H); 1.42-1.37 (m, 1H); 1.29-1.23 (m, 1H); 1.05 (s, 3H); 1.00 (s, 3H).
Thinking_text: This transformation likely involves a conjugate addition of a lithium enolate derived from acetonitrile to a lactone, followed by ring-opening and decarboxylation to yield a γ-ketonitrile. The reactant N-isopropylpropan-2-amine serves as a base to deprotonate acetonitrile, generating a lithium acetylide equivalent via n-butyllithium. The lactone 3,3-dimethyltetrahydropyran-2-one acts as an electrophile, with the carbonyl carbon being attacked by the nucleophilic carbon of the acetylide. The low temperature (-78°C) is crucial to control the highly reactive organolithium species and prevent side reactions. THF is chosen as the solvent due to its ability to solvate lithium ions and stabilize the enolate intermediate. The slow warming allows the ring-opening and subsequent decarboxylation to proceed smoothly. Quenching with ammonium chloride protonates the alkoxide intermediate, and extraction with ethyl acetate isolates the neutral organic product. Therefore, the validated operational sequence is: prepare a base solution in THF at -78°C; add n-butyllithium to generate the acetylide; add acetonitrile; add the lactone solution; stir while warming; quench; extract; wash; dry; concentrate.
